brief: GAP/WHY: Hemolysis newly recognized as side effect of pulsed field ablation (PFA), raising renal safety concerns; broader systemic/thromboinflammatory consequences of PFA-induced hemolysis unknown. OBJECTIVE: assess thromboinflammatory potential of PFA and systemic biological effects of its hemolysis. DESIGN/SETTING: prospective comparative study. POPULATION: 60 patients with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation undergoing pulmonary vein isolation. INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: pentaspline PFA system (n=30) vs radiofrequency ablation (RFA, n=30). METHODS: biomarkers of inflammation, platelet activation, endothelial cell damage, hemolysis, and renal function measured at 3 timepoints; also assessed pericarditis, early recurrences, and 6-month rhythm outcomes. RESULTS: intravascular hemolysis in 100% of PFA patients after mean 42.8 +/- 3.7 deliveries; leukocyte release similar PFA vs RFA (delta 3.0 +/- 2.3 vs 3.6 +/- 2.3 x10^9/L; P=.254); acute platelet activation and endothelial cell damage biomarker changes similar between groups; nitric oxide decreased significantly only in PFA group (-26.5% +/- 17.8%; P<.001); no acute kidney injury occurred, but hemolysis burden significantly associated with creatinine increase, inflammation, and platelet activation; female sex only independent predictor of significant hemolysis after PFA (OR 7.8; 95% CI 1.2-50.2; P=.031); in-hospital pericarditis lower with PFA (10.0% vs 33.3%; P=.028); early recurrence 20.0% vs 26.7% and late recurrence 6.7% vs 10.0% (all P>.05, similar). TAKEAWAY: PFA produces thromboinflammatory reaction similar to RFA; PFA-induced hemolysis occurs in all patients even with limited deliveries and is significantly associated with renal function decline, inflammation, and platelet activation; females at higher risk of significant hemolysis after PFA.
